brief: GAP/RATIONALE: Distinguishing nearfield vs farfield electrograms is essential for mapping/ablating arrhythmias and confirming PVI; current assessment is subjective, relying on perceived electrogram 'sharpness' of pulmonary vein (PV) signals; need a quantitative metric. Electrogram peak frequency (PF) proposed as objective measure of signal proximity. OBJECTIVE: Test whether PF separates nearfield from farfield signals and whether it can confirm PV isolation (PVI) intraprocedurally. DESIGN/SETTING: Prospective cohort; mapping study during PVI procedures in AF patients; left atrial and PV PF maps generated before and after PVI. POPULATION/N: 30 total AF patients undergoing PVI, split into 3 cohorts of 10. Cohort 1 = first 10 paroxysmal AF (cutoff derivation). Cohort 2 = 10 paroxysmal AF (validation). Cohort 3 = 10 persistent AF (validation). INTERVENTION/COMPARISON: PF analysis of electrograms; comparison of PF values between farfield and nearfield signals; derived cutoff tested across cohorts (no device/drug arm — diagnostic accuracy study). KEY RESULTS (exact numbers): PF lower in farfield vs nearfield electrograms. Chosen PF cutoff = 300 Hz to differentiate farfield from nearfield. Sensitivity: cohort 1 = 93.2% (95% CI 81.3-98.6); cohort 2 = 90.0% (95% CI 76.3-97.2); cohort 3 = 98.6% (95% CI 90.1-99.7). Specificity = 100.0% (95% CI 98.2-100.0). AUC (ROC) = 0.99 (95% CI 0.97-1.00) across all patients. TAKEAWAY: Hypothesis confirmed — PF reliably distinguishes nearfield from farfield electrograms and improves recognition of PVI; PF-guided mapping/ablation strategies warrant further pursuit to improve ablation outcomes.
